principle: Update the policy by score-function ascent where each step's gradient is weighted by the empirical fraction of historical bivariate cumulative-return points weakly dominated (with at least one coordinate strict, i.e., the standard Pareto-dominance relation) by the current rollout's cumulative-return point at the same trajectory time index.
primitive_name: Per-time-index empirical bivariate cumulative-return measure mu_hat_t
primitive_type: (time-index t) -> empirical probability measure mu_hat_t : Borel(R^2) -> [0,1] supported on a ring buffer of past bivariate cumulative-return pairs (M^1_t, M^2_t); evaluated by weak-dominance-with-at-least-one-strict counting (the standard Pareto-dominance relation: z prec y iff z^1 <= y^1 and z^2 <= y^2 and z != y) on the realized rollout to produce per-step gradient weight p_t = mu_hat_t({z in R^2 : z preceq y_t(tau), z != y_t(tau)}) in [0,1]
feedback_signal: per-component vector reward read from info['vector'] each step (m=2 channels: treasure, step penalty); cumulative per-channel values M^c_t are jointly inserted into per-t ring buffers as bivariate points (M^1_t, M^2_t) and used to compute weak-Pareto-dominance counts (with at least one coordinate strict) of the current rollout's bivariate cumulative-return point at each trajectory time
novelty_boundary: p_t is rank-invariant under componentwise strictly-monotone reparametrization of either channel: weak dominance and the not-equal clause are both preserved by componentwise strictly-monotone maps, so p_t is unchanged under r^c_t -> phi^c(r^c_t), while every linear or non-linear scalarization f(r^1, r^2) that is monotone in either channel changes under such reparametrization (counterexample: r^1_t -> log(1 + exp(r^1_t)) leaves p_t fixed but changes any wTr); p_t is also a per-step weight on a single on-policy rollout (not a per-trajectory rank like ordergrad), uses joint bivariate Pareto-dominance (not marginal-rank disagreement like COPDEV which is symmetric and has no Pareto direction), uses the full historical buffer (not just a non-dominated subset like PRISM), is a continuous gradient weight (not a discrete selection or front-rank like NSGA-II), and is computed without any learned value, distributional, or hypervolume object. The dominance relation (z preceq y, z != y) is the standard Pareto-dominance from Deb 2002, applied here as a per-step empirical-frequency count, not as a population selection operator.
empirical_claim: On the quick stage (deep-sea-treasure-concave-v0; vector reward), PARGRAD produces (a) gradnorm_var > 0.1 within the first 50 episodes (replicating COPDEV's training-dynamics signal of mechanism presence), (b) an upward drift in the per-rollout mean_pt over training (mean_pt rising from ~0.25 at random init toward ~0.4+ as the policy concentrates on bivariate-dominating trajectories), distinguishing PARGRAD's directional Pareto-progress from COPDEV's symmetric rank-disagreement, AND (c) a final DST-concave score above the random-baseline floor of 194. Under weak-dominance-with-at-least-one-strict, on DST-concave (deterministic channel 2 given survival), p_t reduces to (1/N_t) * |{j : M^1_{t,j} < M^1_t(tau)}| restricted to alive-at-t buffer entries -- a non-trivial channel-1-strict alive-and-lower count whose drift is the substrate signature of Pareto-progress. (Note: under strict componentwise dominance the reduction would have killed p_t identically on DST-concave; the weak-with-one-strict relation -- the standard Pareto-dominance -- is what restores load-bearing behavior.)
falsifier: (Primary, presence) If PARGRAD's gradnorm_var equals the random-uniform-rank ablation's within seed variance during the first 50 episodes, the per-step weight is empirically constant across t and the primitive is decorative. (Secondary, directionality) If PARGRAD's mean_pt_trend does not drift above 0.5 over training -- even when gradnorm_var is non-zero -- the Pareto-direction property of the primitive is not load-bearing in practice and PARGRAD inherits COPDEV's alive-but-not-useful status. (Tertiary, substrate signal) If PARGRAD's final DST-concave score matches random (194) within seed variance, the Pareto-rank gradient weight produces no measurable shift in policy return on this substrate within the budget. (Quaternary, bivariate-vs-unicriterial) If PARGRAD matches the unicriterial channel-1-only sanity ablation on mean_pt_trend and final DST-concave score within seed variance, the bivariate primitive collapses to a single-channel rank weight on this deterministic-channel-2 substrate -- a neutral but informative outcome that demands a non-degenerate vector substrate before claiming directional Pareto-progress.
ablation_plan: Primary ablation: replace the per-step Pareto-rank weight p_t with a fresh-per-step uniform random sample p_t ~ Uniform(0, 1). Concretely in train_ablate.py: skip the dominance count (do not read ring_buffer[t]), sample p_t independently per step from Uniform(0, 1), skip ring-buffer maintenance, apply the same score-function update Sigma_t p_t * grad log pi(a_t|s_t). All other code (rollout, policy net, optimizer, hyperparameters, gradient-norm logging) is preserved. This preserves the per-step weight non-uniformity (so gradnorm_var stays non-zero in the ablation) but kills the systematic Pareto-direction. Secondary sanity ablation (retained per Reviewer): unicriterial channel-1-only rank -- maintain a single-channel ring_buffer1[t] of past M^1_{t,j} only, compute p_t = (1/N_t)*|{j : M^1_{t,j} < M^1_t(tau)}|, apply the same score-function update; this tests whether the bivariate (joint) structure adds anything above a marginal channel-1 rank weight on this deterministic-channel-2 substrate. Discriminating logged observables in all three arms: gradnorm_var (presence; non-zero in PARGRAD and random-uniform), mean_pt per rollout, mean_pt_trend across episodes (in random-uniform arm logged via shadow buffer for diagnostic comparison; should drift upward in PARGRAD, hover near 0.5 in random-uniform; behavior in unicriterial arm settles whether bivariate structure is load-bearing here), mean episode length, final hypervolume on DST-concave.
proof_debt: (1) Two-timescale Borkar 2008-style convergence of theta_n with the rolling-buffer of bivariate cumulative-return pairs on the slow timescale, identifying the stationary objective J_tilde(pi) = E_tau[Sigma_t F_tilde^pi_t(y_t(tau))] where F_tilde^pi_t(y) = P_{Z ~ nu^pi_t}(Z preceq y, Z != y) is the bivariate Pareto-dominance probability of the policy-induced cumulative-return distribution at trajectory time t. (2) Pareto-improvement claim: do stationary maximizers of J_tilde correspond to Pareto-non-dominated policies in the achievable bivariate vector-return set? Conjecture yes, by a bivariate-stochastic-dominance fixed-point argument. (3) Bias and variance of the bivariate empirical measure on a finite ring buffer (Vapnik-Chervonenkis VC-dim 4 for rectangles in R^2) and propagation to gradient bias. (4) Degenerate-channel reduction on DST-concave: under weak-dominance-with-at-least-one-strict and deterministic channel 2 given survival, p_t reduces to a channel-1-strict alive-and-lower count; characterize when this reduction remains a genuine bivariate primitive (non-degenerate vector substrate retest) vs. is operationally a single-channel rank weight (settled empirically by the unicriterial ablation). (5) Connection to bivariate stochastic dominance theory (Atkinson-Bourguignon 1982; Hadar-Russell 1974).
